name: doona
title: doona
summary: doona - Network fuzzer forked from bed

commands.name: doona

arguments.description: Execute a health check after every <int> fuzz cases
arguments.raw: -c  Execute a health check after every <int> fuzz cases

arguments.description: Dump test case to stdout (use in combination with -r)
arguments.raw: -d  Dump test case to stdout (use in combination with -r)

arguments.description: Print help (this text).
Keep trying until server passes a health check
arguments.raw: -k  Keep trying until server passes a health check

arguments.description: Exit after executing <num> number of fuzz cases
arguments.raw: -M  Exit after executing <num> number of fuzz cases

arguments.description: Possible values for <module>: DICT, FINGER, FTP, HTTP, HTTP_MORE, HTTP_SP, HTTP_WEBDAV,
arguments.raw: -m  Possible values for <module>: DICT, FINGER, FTP, HTTP, HTTP_MORE, HTTP_SP, HTTP_WEBDAV,

arguments.description: seconds to wait after each test (default: 2 seconds)
arguments.raw: -o  seconds to wait after each test (default: 2 seconds)

arguments.description: Port to connect to (default: module specific standard port)
arguments.raw: -p  Port to connect to (default: module specific standard port)

arguments.description: Resumes fuzzing at test case index
arguments.raw: -r  Resumes fuzzing at test case index

arguments.description: Host to check (default: localhost)

commands.raw_help:
root@kali:~# doona -h
 
Doona 1.0 by Wireghoul (www.justanotherhacker.com)
 
 Usage:
 
./doona.pl -m [module] <options>
 
-m <module>   = DICT/FINGER/FTP/HTTP/HTTP_MORE/HTTP_SP/HTTP_WEBDAV/IMAP/IRC/LPD/NNTP/PJL/POP/PROXY/RTSP/SMTP/SOCKS4/SOCKS5/TFTP/WHOIS
-c <int>      = Execute a health check after every <int> fuzz cases
-t <target>   = Host to check (default: localhost)
-p <port>     = Port to connect to (default: module specific standard port)
-o <timeout>  = seconds to wait after each test (default: 2 seconds)
-r <index>    = Resumes fuzzing at test case index
-k            = Keep trying until server passes a health check
-d            = Dump test case to stdout (use in combination with -r)
-M <num>      = Exit after executing <num> number of fuzz cases
-h            = Help (this text)
use "./doona.pl -m [module] -h" for module specific option.
 
Only -m is a mandatory switch.

examples.command:
doona -m HTTP -t 192.168.1.15 -M 5

 Doona 1.0 by Wireghoul (www.justanotherhacker.com)

 + Buffer overflow testing
    1/37   [XAXAX] ......
Max requests (5) completed, index: 5

local_help.man:
DOONA(1)                         User Commands                         DOONA(1)

NAME
     doona - Network fuzzer forked from bed

SYNOPSIS
     doona -m [module] <options>

OPTIONS
     -m <module>
            Possible  values  for <module>: DICT, FINGER, FTP, HTTP, HTTP_MORE,
            HTTP_SP, HTTP_WEBDAV, IMAP, IRC, LPD, NNTP, PJL, POP, PROXY,  RTSP,
            SMTP, SOCKS4, SOCKS5, TFTP, WHOIS

     -c <int>
            Execute a health check after every <int> fuzz cases

     -t <target>
            Host to check (default: localhost)

     -p <port>
            Port to connect to (default: module specific standard port)

     -o <timeout>
            seconds to wait after each test (default: 2 seconds)

     -r <index>
            Resumes fuzzing at test case index

     -k     Keep trying until server passes a health check

     -d     Dump test case to stdout (use in combination with -r)

     -M <num>
            Exit after executing <num> number of fuzz cases

     -h     Print help (this text).

            Use "doona -m [module] -h" for module specific option.

            Only -m is a mandatory switch.

doona                             January 2019                         DOONA(1)
local_help.apropos: doona (1)            - Network fuzzer forked from bed

binary_metadata.file_type: Bourne-Again shell script, ASCII text executable

apt_metadata.description:
Network fuzzer forked from bed
Doona is a fork of the Bruteforce Exploit Detector Tool (BED).
BED is a program which is designed to check daemons for
potential buffer overflows, format string bugs etc.

use_cases: When you need Kali Linux, use doona because doona - Network fuzzer forked from bed.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `doona -m [module] <options>` as documented upstream for doona.
When chaining Kali Linux tools, pair doona with sibling catalog entries sharing its attack_surface/phase_hint.
When validating lab setup, run `doona --help` (or the README entrypoint) and confirm required hardware/deps from the catalog.
